question: What is the difference between 'struct' and 'class' in C++?
options: There is no difference
Members of struct are public by default; members of class are private by default
Struct cannot have member functions; class can
Class cannot be inherited; struct can
correct_answer: Members of struct are public by default; members of class are private by default

question: What is RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) in C++?
options: A design pattern for creating singletons
A principle where resource lifetime is tied to object lifetime – resources are acquired in the constructor and released in the destructor
A way to initialize arrays at runtime
An acronym for a C++ compiler optimization
correct_answer: A principle where resource lifetime is tied to object lifetime – resources are acquired in the constructor and released in the destructor

question: What is the difference between 'new' and 'malloc' in C++?
options: They are identical
new calls the constructor and is type-safe; malloc only allocates raw memory without calling constructors
malloc is faster than new
new is only for arrays; malloc is for single objects
correct_answer: new calls the constructor and is type-safe; malloc only allocates raw memory without calling constructors

question: What are smart pointers in C++?
options: Pointers that can point to multiple objects
RAII-based wrapper classes (unique_ptr, shared_ptr, weak_ptr) that automatically manage dynamic memory lifetime
Pointers with built-in encryption
Pointers optimized for speed
correct_answer: RAII-based wrapper classes (unique_ptr, shared_ptr, weak_ptr) that automatically manage dynamic memory lifetime

question: What is the difference between 'unique_ptr' and 'shared_ptr'?
options: They are the same
unique_ptr has exclusive ownership (non-copyable); shared_ptr uses reference counting for shared ownership
shared_ptr is faster than unique_ptr
unique_ptr can be copied; shared_ptr cannot
correct_answer: unique_ptr has exclusive ownership (non-copyable); shared_ptr uses reference counting for shared ownership

question: What is a virtual function in C++?
options: A function that does not exist
A member function declared with 'virtual' that enables runtime polymorphism through dynamic dispatch
A function that can only be called from virtual machines
A function with no implementation
correct_answer: A member function declared with 'virtual' that enables runtime polymorphism through dynamic dispatch

question: What is a pure virtual function?
options: A virtual function with no side effects
A virtual function declared with '= 0', making the class abstract and requiring derived classes to provide an implementation
A virtual function that is very efficient
A function that cannot be overridden
correct_answer: A virtual function declared with '= 0', making the class abstract and requiring derived classes to provide an implementation

question: What is the 'vtable' (virtual table) in C++?
options: A table of variable names
A compiler-generated table of function pointers used to resolve virtual function calls at runtime
A table of template parameters
A lookup table for variable types
correct_answer: A compiler-generated table of function pointers used to resolve virtual function calls at runtime

question: What is move semantics in C++11?
options: Moving files in the filesystem
A mechanism to transfer ownership of resources from one object to another without copying, using rvalue references (&&)
Moving objects between threads
A compiler optimization for loop unrolling
correct_answer: A mechanism to transfer ownership of resources from one object to another without copying, using rvalue references (&&)

question: What is the difference between an lvalue and an rvalue in C++?
options: lvalue is a left-side value; rvalue is always a number
An lvalue has a persistent address (can appear on the left of =); an rvalue is a temporary with no persistent address
There is no difference in C++
lvalue is for local variables; rvalue is for remote values
correct_answer: An lvalue has a persistent address (can appear on the left of =); an rvalue is a temporary with no persistent address

question: What is 'std::move' used for in C++?
options: To physically move data in memory
To cast an lvalue to an rvalue reference, enabling move semantics
To move a thread to another CPU core
To move files between directories
correct_answer: To cast an lvalue to an rvalue reference, enabling move semantics

question: What is a lambda expression in C++11?
options: A Greek letter used in variable names
An anonymous function object that can capture variables from its enclosing scope
A special template syntax
A type of exception
correct_answer: An anonymous function object that can capture variables from its enclosing scope

question: What is the Rule of Five in C++?
options: A rule about maximum function parameters
If a class defines any of destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, or move assignment, it should define all five
A rule about maximum inheritance depth
A coding style guideline about five spaces of indentation
correct_answer: If a class defines any of destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, or move assignment, it should define all five

question: What is 'constexpr' in C++11?
options: A keyword to make variables constant at runtime
A keyword indicating that a function or variable can be evaluated at compile time
A keyword for constant expressions in exceptions
A deprecated keyword
correct_answer: A keyword indicating that a function or variable can be evaluated at compile time

question: What is the difference between 'const' and 'constexpr'?
options: They are identical
const means the value won't change after initialization; constexpr means the value must be computable at compile time
constexpr is for functions only; const is for variables only
const is C++11; constexpr is C++17
correct_answer: const means the value won't change after initialization; constexpr means the value must be computable at compile time

question: What is template metaprogramming in C++?
options: Using templates to generate HTML
Using C++ templates to perform computation at compile time rather than runtime
Programming with metal templates
Using templates for dynamic typing
correct_answer: Using C++ templates to perform computation at compile time rather than runtime

question: What is SFINAE in C++?
options: A compiler optimization technique
Substitution Failure Is Not An Error – when template argument substitution fails, the template is silently discarded
A standard library algorithm
A memory allocation strategy
correct_answer: Substitution Failure Is Not An Error – when template argument substitution fails, the template is silently discarded

question: What is the purpose of 'std::forward' in C++?
options: To forward function calls to another thread
To achieve perfect forwarding – preserving the value category (lvalue/rvalue) of a template argument
To forward network packets
To forward exceptions
correct_answer: To achieve perfect forwarding – preserving the value category (lvalue/rvalue) of a template argument

question: What is 'auto' keyword used for in C++11?
options: To declare automatic storage class variables
To let the compiler deduce the type of a variable from its initializer
To automatically optimize code
To create automatic backups
correct_answer: To let the compiler deduce the type of a variable from its initializer

question: What is the difference between 'override' and 'final' keywords in C++11?
options: They are the same
override ensures a function overrides a virtual base method; final prevents further overriding or inheritance
final overrides; override finalizes
override is for variables; final is for functions
correct_answer: override ensures a function overrides a virtual base method; final prevents further overriding or inheritance

question: What is the diamond problem in C++ inheritance?
options: A problem with diamond-shaped data structures
Ambiguity when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from the same base class, resolved with virtual inheritance
A performance issue with multiple inheritance
A memory leak pattern
correct_answer: Ambiguity when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from the same base class, resolved with virtual inheritance

question: What is the Standard Template Library (STL)?
options: A library for string templates
A collection of generic containers, algorithms, iterators, and function objects in C++
A library for template emails
A library for SQL template queries
correct_answer: A collection of generic containers, algorithms, iterators, and function objects in C++

question: What is the difference between 'std::vector' and 'std::array'?
options: They are identical
vector is dynamically sized (heap allocated); array has a fixed size known at compile time (stack allocated)
array is dynamic; vector is fixed
vector stores only integers; array stores any type
correct_answer: vector is dynamically sized (heap allocated); array has a fixed size known at compile time (stack allocated)

question: What is 'std::unordered_map' and how does it differ from 'std::map'?
options: They are the same container
unordered_map uses a hash table (O(1) average lookup); map uses a balanced BST (O(log n) lookup, ordered keys)
map is faster than unordered_map
unordered_map is sorted; map is unsorted
correct_answer: unordered_map uses a hash table (O(1) average lookup); map uses a balanced BST (O(log n) lookup, ordered keys)

question: What is exception handling in C++ using try-catch?
options: A way to handle syntax errors
A mechanism to handle runtime errors by throwing exceptions and catching them in catch blocks
A way to catch compiler warnings
A logging mechanism
correct_answer: A mechanism to handle runtime errors by throwing exceptions and catching them in catch blocks

question: What is the 'noexcept' specifier in C++?
options: A way to suppress all exceptions
A specifier indicating a function is guaranteed not to throw exceptions, enabling compiler optimizations
A debugging flag
A keyword to catch exceptions silently
correct_answer: A specifier indicating a function is guaranteed not to throw exceptions, enabling compiler optimizations

question: What is 'static_cast' vs 'dynamic_cast' vs 'reinterpret_cast'?
options: They all do the same thing
static_cast: compile-time, no runtime check; dynamic_cast: runtime-checked polymorphic cast; reinterpret_cast: low-level bit reinterpretation
dynamic_cast is for dynamic memory; static_cast is for static memory
reinterpret_cast is the safest
correct_answer: static_cast: compile-time, no runtime check; dynamic_cast: runtime-checked polymorphic cast; reinterpret_cast: low-level bit reinterpretation

question: What is a 'std::thread' in C++11?
options: A string of text
A class representing a single thread of execution for concurrent programming
A threading needle metaphor
A deprecated C++ feature
correct_answer: A class representing a single thread of execution for concurrent programming

question: What is 'std::mutex' used for?
options: To mute audio output
A mutual exclusion primitive to protect shared data from concurrent access by multiple threads
To manage memory usage tracking
To create mutable variables
correct_answer: A mutual exclusion primitive to protect shared data from concurrent access by multiple threads

question: What is 'std::lock_guard' in C++?
options: A security guard for locks
An RAII wrapper that acquires a mutex in its constructor and releases it in its destructor, preventing lock leaks
A guard condition for loops
A file locking mechanism
correct_answer: An RAII wrapper that acquires a mutex in its constructor and releases it in its destructor, preventing lock leaks

question: What is 'std::atomic' used for in C++?
options: To create very small variables
To provide lock-free, thread-safe operations on variables without requiring mutexes
To create atomic energy simulations
To create immutable variables
correct_answer: To provide lock-free, thread-safe operations on variables without requiring mutexes

question: What is the copy-and-swap idiom in C++?
options: A sorting algorithm
A technique for implementing exception-safe copy assignment by creating a temporary copy and swapping
A method to copy files and swap names
A technique for swapping pointers
correct_answer: A technique for implementing exception-safe copy assignment by creating a temporary copy and swapping

question: What is the purpose of the 'explicit' keyword in C++?
options: To explicitly allocate memory
To prevent implicit type conversions in constructors and conversion operators
To explicitly call destructors
To make variables explicitly typed
correct_answer: To prevent implicit type conversions in constructors and conversion operators

question: What are variadic templates in C++11?
options: Templates with variable types
Templates that accept a variable number of template arguments using parameter packs (...)
Templates that vary at runtime
Templates for variable declarations
correct_answer: Templates that accept a variable number of template arguments using parameter packs (...)

question: What is 'std::optional' (C++17)?
options: A way to make parameters optional
A wrapper that may or may not contain a value, representing an optional value without using pointers or sentinel values
An optional compiler flag
A nullable reference type
correct_answer: A wrapper that may or may not contain a value, representing an optional value without using pointers or sentinel values

question: What is 'std::variant' (C++17)?
options: A variable with multiple names
A type-safe union that can hold one of several specified types at a time
A variant of the string class
A variable-length array
correct_answer: A type-safe union that can hold one of several specified types at a time

question: What is the pimpl (Pointer to Implementation) idiom?
options: A simple pointer pattern
A technique to hide implementation details by storing them behind a pointer to a private class, reducing compilation dependencies
A performance improvement technique
A pattern for implementing polymorphism
correct_answer: A technique to hide implementation details by storing them behind a pointer to a private class, reducing compilation dependencies

question: What is the Curiously Recurring Template Pattern (CRTP)?
options: A bug in recursive templates
A pattern where a class derives from a template base class using itself as a template parameter, enabling static polymorphism
A curious naming convention
A pattern for creating recursive data structures
correct_answer: A pattern where a class derives from a template base class using itself as a template parameter, enabling static polymorphism

question: What is 'decltype' used for in C++11?
options: To declare types explicitly
To query the type of an expression at compile time
To decline type checking
To declare template parameters
correct_answer: To query the type of an expression at compile time

question: What is a 'std::function' in C++?
options: A standard way to define functions
A general-purpose polymorphic function wrapper that can store any callable object (functions, lambdas, functors)
A function that follows the standard
A standard library function
correct_answer: A general-purpose polymorphic function wrapper that can store any callable object (functions, lambdas, functors)

question: What is the difference between deep copy and shallow copy in C++?
options: They are identical
Deep copy duplicates the underlying data; shallow copy copies only the pointer/reference, sharing the same data
Shallow copy is safer than deep copy
Deep copy is only for primitive types
correct_answer: Deep copy duplicates the underlying data; shallow copy copies only the pointer/reference, sharing the same data

question: What are namespaces in C++ used for?
options: To name files
To organize code and prevent name collisions by grouping identifiers into named scopes
To create network addresses
To define variable types
correct_answer: To organize code and prevent name collisions by grouping identifiers into named scopes

question: What is 'std::condition_variable' used for?
options: To check variable conditions at compile time
A synchronization primitive that allows threads to wait until notified by another thread that a condition is met
To define conditional compilation
To validate variable types
correct_answer: A synchronization primitive that allows threads to wait until notified by another thread that a condition is met

question: What is 'std::async' and 'std::future' in C++?
options: Asynchronous file I/O functions
std::async launches a task asynchronously; std::future provides a mechanism to retrieve the result when ready
They are identical to std::thread
Async networking primitives
correct_answer: std::async launches a task asynchronously; std::future provides a mechanism to retrieve the result when ready

question: What is 'enum class' (scoped enum) in C++11?
options: A class that contains only enums
A strongly-typed enum that doesn't implicitly convert to int and has its own scope, preventing name collisions
An enum stored in a class file
A deprecated way to define enums
correct_answer: A strongly-typed enum that doesn't implicitly convert to int and has its own scope, preventing name collisions

question: What is a dangling reference in C++?
options: A reference to a global variable
A reference that refers to an object that has been destroyed or gone out of scope, leading to undefined behavior
A reference that is NULL
A reference to a constant value
correct_answer: A reference that refers to an object that has been destroyed or gone out of scope, leading to undefined behavior

question: What is 'std::string_view' (C++17)?
options: A mutable string class
A lightweight, non-owning reference to a string (or substring) that avoids copying
A string formatting view
A graphical view of string data
correct_answer: A lightweight, non-owning reference to a string (or substring) that avoids copying

question: What is the difference between 'static_cast' and 'reinterpret_cast' in C++?
options: They are identical in behavior
static_cast performs type-safe conversions at compile time; reinterpret_cast performs low-level bit reinterpretation without type safety
reinterpret_cast is safer than static_cast
static_cast only works with pointers
correct_answer: static_cast performs type-safe conversions at compile time; reinterpret_cast performs low-level bit reinterpretation without type safety

question: What is CRTP (Curiously Recurring Template Pattern) in C++?
options: A runtime polymorphism pattern using virtual functions
A pattern where a class derives from a template instantiation of itself, enabling static (compile-time) polymorphism
A pattern for creating recursive data structures
A method to cast templates at runtime
correct_answer: A pattern where a class derives from a template instantiation of itself, enabling static (compile-time) polymorphism

question: What does the 'noexcept' specifier indicate in C++?
options: The function will catch all exceptions internally
The function is guaranteed not to throw exceptions, allowing compiler optimizations
The function disables exception handling globally
The function will retry on exception
correct_answer: The function is guaranteed not to throw exceptions, allowing compiler optimizations
